stem: By the Fundamental Theorem of Finite Abelian Groups, the number of ABELIAN groups of order \( [[n]] = [[p]]^{[[a]]} \) (up to isomorphism) equals the number of ways to write the exponent [[a]] as a sum of positive integers (an integer partition of [[a]]). How many abelian groups of order [[n]] are there, up to isomorphism?
solution: The abelian groups of order \( p^{a} \) correspond exactly to the partitions of the exponent [[a]] (each partition \( a=\lambda_1+\lambda_2+\dots \) gives \( \mathbb{Z}_{p^{\lambda_1}}\times\mathbb{Z}_{p^{\lambda_2}}\times\cdots \)). Here that count is \( p([[a]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This just restates the exponent [[a]] instead of counting its partitions.
This is one less than the exponent, not the number of partitions.

This is the number of DIVISORS of \( [[p]]^{[[a]]} \) (i.e., \( [[a]]+1 \)), which counts subgroups of \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[p]]^{[[a]]}} \), not the number of isomorphism types of abelian groups of that order.
This mistakenly doubles the partition count with each unit increase in the exponent, like counting subsets rather than partitions.
This uses the triangular-number formula, an unrelated count.
This overshoots the correct partition count by 2.
This overshoots the correct partition count by 3.

stem: The integer \( [[n]] = [[p]]^{[[a]]} \cdot [[q]]^{[[b]]} \) factors into the distinct primes [[p]] and [[q]]. By the Fundamental Theorem of Finite Abelian Groups, the number of abelian groups of order [[n]] (up to isomorphism) is the PRODUCT of the number of partitions of each prime's exponent. How many abelian groups of order [[n]] are there, up to isomorphism?
solution: Since [[p]] and [[q]] are distinct primes, the count of abelian groups of order \( [[p]]^{[[a]]}[[q]]^{[[b]]} \) is the product of the partition counts for each exponent: \( p([[a]])\cdot p([[b]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This adds the two partition counts instead of multiplying them.
This multiplies the exponents themselves, not the partition counts.
This uses \( (a+1)(b+1) \), the divisor-count formula, instead of partition counts.
This wrongly combines the exponents into one partition instead of partitioning each prime's exponent separately.
This is off by one from \( p([[a]])p([[b]]) \).
This is off by one from \( p([[a]])p([[b]]) \).

stem: The integer \( [[n]] = [[p]]^{[[a]]}\cdot[[q]]^{[[b]]}\cdot[[r]]^{[[c]]} \) has three distinct prime factors [[p]], [[q]], [[r]]. Using the Fundamental Theorem of Finite Abelian Groups, how many abelian groups of order [[n]] are there, up to isomorphism?
solution: The abelian-group count factors over the distinct primes, one partition-count factor per prime power: \( p([[a]])\cdot p([[b]])\cdot p([[c]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This adds the three partition counts instead of multiplying them.
This multiplies the exponents directly instead of their partition counts.
This uses the divisor-count formula \( (a+1)(b+1)(c+1) \) instead of partition counts.
This wrongly partitions the combined exponent \( a+b+c \) instead of each prime's exponent separately.
This is off by one from the correct product of partition counts.
This is off by one from the correct product of partition counts.
